title-group.article-title: Take charge after long COVID: a mixed methods randomised controlled pilot study protocol

contrib-group.aff: aDivision of Rehabilitation, Aged and Palliative Care, Southern Adelaide Local Health Network, Bedford Park, Australia
bCollege of Medicine and Public Health, Flinders University, Adelaide, Australia
cCollege of Education, Psychology & Social Work, Flinders University, Adelaide, Australia
dJohn Walsh Centre for Rehabilitation Research, Northern Sydney Local Health District, St Leonards, Australia
eFaculty of Medicine and Health, University of Sydney, Sydney, Australia
fMedical Research Institute of New Zealand, Wellington, New Zealand
gCollege of Nursing and Health Sciences, Flinders University, Adelaide, Australia

fn.p: Supplemental data for this article can be accessed online at https://doi.org/10.1080/07853890.2025.2516694.

author-notes.corresp: CONTACT Subbuh Luker subbuh.luker@sa.gov.au Division of Rehabilitation, Aged Care and Palliative Care, Southern Adelaide Local Health Network, Level 4, Rehabilitation and Palliative Care Building, FMC, Flinders Drive, Bedford Park, SA 5042 Australia.

event.event-desc: published online in a building issue

permissions.copyright-statement: © 2025 The Author(s). Published by Informa UK Limited, trading as Taylor & Francis Group

license.license-p: This is an Open Access article distributed under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial License (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/4.0/), which permits unrestricted non-commercial use, distribution, and reproduction in any medium, provided the original work is properly cited. The terms on which this article has been published allow the posting of the Accepted Manuscript in a repository by the author(s) or with their consent.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Post COVID-19 condition is a debilitating illness with over 200 symptoms across 10 organ systems and is presently impacting millions worldwide. The National Institute for Health and Care Excellence recommends a multidisciplinary treatment approach including person-centred self-management strategies, however evidence for specific programs is lacking. The Take Charge intervention is a person-centred, self-management rehabilitation approach that has been effective in recovery after stroke, but not yet tested in post COVID-19 condition.

sec.title: Methods & analysis
sec.p: A prospective, single-centre, parallel, 2 group, mixed methods, randomized controlled trial with embedded process evaluation of the Take Charge intervention in individuals living with post COVID-19 condition. Participants will be at least 18 years of age, have a confirmed diagnosis of post COVID-19 condition with ongoing symptoms, and be known to a hospital clinic for assessment and treatment of patients with post-acute sequelae of COVID-19. The primary outcomes are the Modified COVID-19 Yorkshire Rehabilitation Scale and the COVID-19 Core Outcome Measure for Recovery. The secondary outcomes include physical and self-report measures, and feasibility measures. Qualitative interviews will also be conducted to understand the clinicians’ and participants’ experiences. Statistical analysis will be performed on an intention-to-treat basis using a multivariate mixed-effect linear regression model.

sec.title: Ethics & dissemination
sec.p: This study adheres to the Declaration of Helsinki. This study was approved by the Southern Adelaide Clinical Human Research Ethics Committee (approval number: 2022/SSA00695/OFR: 219.22, protocol version 3.3 19 February 2024). The results will be disseminated in peer-reviewed journals, conference presentations, and media.

institution-wrap.institution: Royal Australasian College of Physicians Foundation

funding-group.funding-statement: This research was supported by grant funding from The Flinders Foundation, The Hospital Research Foundation Group and Southern Adelaide Local Health Network’s ‘Enquiry Grant Round’. Author S.L. was supported by an Australasian Faculty of Rehabilitation Medicine ‘Research Entry Scholarship’ from the Royal Australasian College of Physicians Foundation. The funding bodies did not have any role in the study design, data collection, management, analysis, or interpretation of the results.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: The SARS CoV-2 (COVID-19) pandemic has affected hundreds of millions of people worldwide [1]. Most people infected with COVID-19 fully recover, however, evidence suggests that at least 10% experience various mid- and long-term effects [2,3]. These mid- and long-term effects are collectively known as post COVID-19 condition, commonly referred to as ‘Long COVID’ and have a profound impact on quality of life [4]. Post COVID-19 condition occurs in individuals with a history of probable or confirmed COVID-19 infection, usually within three months of the acute infection, with symptoms and effects that last for at least two months and cannot be explained by an alternative diagnosis [4,5]. The Institute for Health Metrics and Evaluation estimated that by the end of 2021, 3.92 billion individuals had been infected with COVID-19 and that 144.7 million developed post COVID-19 condition [4].

With more than 200 symptoms across 10 organ systems, the most common are fatigue, breathlessness, and cognitive dysfunction, which are often significant enough to impact activities of daily living [2–4,6,7]. There does not appear to be a relationship between the initial severity of COVID-19 and the likelihood of developing post COVID-19 condition and hence, all infected individuals are at risk [2,7]. Symptoms may persist from acute illness or develop after the initial recovery and may fluctuate or relapse over time [8]. Evidence indicates that this population is relatively young (with an average age of 46.4 years), resulting in a considerable impact on the global workforce [8]. One study reported that 45% of post COVID-19 condition respondents required a reduced work schedule and 22% were completely unable to work [7].

With no established curative pharmacological or non-pharmacological treatment available, the World Health Organization recommends holistic care including rehabilitation [4]. The National Institute for Health and Care Excellence recommends providing advice on self-management strategies to people with post COVID-19 condition [6,9], However, there is no clear consensus regarding which self-management approach should be offered and how to provide this [9]. In summary, there is a significant risk that individuals with post COVID-19 condition will continue to experience encumbering symptoms and remain out of the workforce. There is sustained public and expert interest in this area given its large-scale consequences and it is imperative that researchers continue to investigate interventions that may improve outcomes [10].

This study aims to test an intervention known as Take Charge which aims to promote hope, activity and participation and differs from many forms of ‘standard’ practice [11,12]. It is a short (two sessions) therapeutic intervention that encourages people with chronic health conditions to consider their values, what is important to them, and how they will maximize their quality of life in the coming months. It is designed to de-medicalize a condition, improve intrinsic motivation, and foster the participant to Take Charge of their own recovery [11,12]. Few interventions have been able to achieve this or have required time-intensive programs and extensive training, which Take Charge does not. The intervention was successful in two randomized controlled trials comprising 572 stroke patients, where it was shown to improve quality of life, reduce carer strain, and increase independence up to 12 months post-intervention [11,13]. The intervention is brief, low cost, requires minimal training, and can be delivered face-to-face in a hospital outpatient setting or via telehealth [14]. If successful in post COVID-19 condition, it could make an immeasurable difference to quality of life and global economies.

sec.title: Objectives
sec.p:
The objectives of this study are to:
Adapt the Take Charge program for people with post COVID-19 condition.
Determine the effects of the program on health-related quality of life, function in daily tasks and post COVID-19 condition symptoms
Understand the experience of delivering and receiving the program from the perspective of the clinicians and participants and learn of any further suggestions for refinement.
Evaluate the intervention’s feasibility for larger scale research and long-term implementation into clinical practice.

sec.title: Hypothesis
sec.p: We hypothesize that people who receive the Take Charge program will have improved health outcomes (post COVID-19 condition symptoms and health-related quality of life) as well as increased rates of return to work or working hours. We anticipate that participants will report higher levels of quality of life, higher levels of self-efficacy, and increased levels of independence at six months after receiving the intervention compared with those who are offered standard advice for post COVID-19 condition. We hypothesize that the intervention will be acceptable and feasible for participants with post COVID-19 condition and will be suitable for a larger multicentre trial.

sec.title: Methods/design
sec.p: This protocol paper has been written to satisfy the Standard Protocol Items: Recommendations for Interventional Trials (SPIRIT) Statement [15]. The results will be reported in accordance with the Consolidated Standards of Reporting Trials (CONSORT) Statement [16]. Both publications adhere to the Template for Intervention Description and Replication (TIDieR) checklist [17].
Australia and New Zealand Clinical Trial Registry ACTRN12623000077662p
Registered 23/01/2023. https://www.anzctr.org.au/Trial/Registration/TrialReview.aspx?id=385073&isReview=true

sec.title: Study design
sec.p: The study is a prospective, single-centre, parallel, 2 group, mixed methods, randomized (1:1) controlled trial with embedded process evaluation. An overview of the study design is shown in Figure 1. The schedules of enrolment, interventions, and assessments are presented in Table 1.

caption.p: Study timeline including schedule of enrolment, interventions and assessments.

tr.td: Within 2 weeks of baseline assessment (1 to 4 weeks)
PICF mail out and follow up phone call
Postural blood pressure, heart rate, temperature, weight, height
Modified COVID-19 Yorkshire Rehabilitation Scale (17 items)
COVID-19 Core Outcome Measure for Recovery (1 item)
Medical Outcomes Study Short Form 36 (36 items)
Modified Medical Research Council Dyspnoea Scale (1 item)
Work Productivity and Activity Impairment Questionnaire (6 items) and survey of employment status and hours worked per week
Take Charge and control group interviews

sec.title: Setting
sec.p: Potential participants will be identified through the Southern Adelaide Local Health Network Post-Acute Sequelae of COVID-19 (PASC) clinic. Interventions will be delivered within rehabilitation outpatient service spaces or via telehealth.

sec.title: Eligibility criteria
sec.p: Eligible participants will be community-dwelling adults aged 18 years or older who have attended the Southern Adelaide Local Health Network’s PASC clinic and have a confirmed diagnosis of post COVID-19 condition with ongoing symptoms. Patients are referred to the PASC clinic based on their clinical needs by their general practitioner or other medical specialists. Given the low-risk nature of the intervention, the exclusion criteria will be limited to significant or imminently life-limiting conditions or inability to provide informed consent.

sec.title: Participant identification
sec.p:
Two methods will be used to identify potential participants.
Mail invitation to all patients who have attended the PASC clinic

Patients known to the PASC clinic will be mailed study information, inviting them to participate and will be follow up by telephone call.
In person invitation through the PASC clinic
Patients attending the PASC clinic will be provided with written study information when checking in. The treating clinician identifies those who agree to receive a follow-up telephone call.

If a potential participant indicates that they would like to enrol in the study to the researcher, they will be booked to a research intake clinic to confirm eligibility and collect baseline measures. Participants who are unable to attend the intake clinic for any reason (e.g. means of transport, financial constraints, distance, symptom burden, etc.) will not be excluded from the study to ensure inclusivity and provide a diverse real-world’ population, enabling generalizability of results [18,]. These participants will undergo eligibility screening via telehealth and complete self-report outcome measures only.

sec.title: Confirmation of eligibility assessment and consent
sec.p: Participants will be able to sign and return the consent form by email, post, or bring the paper copy to the intake clinic. At the intake clinic, a clinician will collect information regarding age, sex, comorbidities, month and year of initial COVID-19 infection, level of acute and subsequent care required, current medications, and referrals made internally and externally at the PASC clinic. Basic observations will be recorded, including postural blood pressure, heart rate, temperature, and weight. The clinician will review the information and confirm that the participants are eligible to enrol.

sec.title: Randomisation, blinding & allocation concealment
sec.p: Participants will be randomized to the Take Charge intervention or the active control group in a 1:1 allocation ratio. Randomization will occur in block sizes of 2 or 4, and will not be stratified. The randomization sequence will be developed by a health and scientific data expert external to the research trial and will be encoded into the REDCap database for use in the trial. The research staff involved in the trial will be blinded to the randomization sequence.

By virtue of design participants are only able to be blinded for their baseline physical and self report measures after which they will be advised of their group allocation. The clinician collecting the physical baseline, 3 and 6 month measures will be blinded to group allocation.

sec.title: Intervention
sec.p: Both groups will continue to receive standard clinical care, which includes medical, nursing, and physiotherapist assessment at their initial PASC clinic appointment, followed by further appointments based on clinical needs in line with standard practice. If indicated, patients are referred to medical specialists (such as a cardiologist or respiratory physician) as well as allied health professionals, including occupational therapy, physiotherapy, exercise physiology, and/or a specific rehabilitation program. The clinic offers reviews and inputs, as required by these allied health professionals. All participants will be advised that they can continue to access any other usual healthcare, including medications and consultations with health professionals outside the study.

sec.title: Take charge intervention
sec.p: Unlike traditional self-management programs that focus on symptom management, Take Charge aims to de-medicalize and shift focus away from the illness and its symptoms [11,19]. It involves (1) a nondirected exploration of what aspects of life and which people are most important to the person, (2) hopes and fears for the future, and (3) an individualized assessment of areas where they could make progress and personal goal setting using an illustrated workbook, that is, self-directed rehabilitation [11,19]. The workbook allows participants to write down goals and steps, and to see this as an ongoing process, they can review them in their own time and, in essence, ‘Take Charge’ of their own recovery. The workbook was adapted from the original Take Charge intervention for stroke patients [13,20] by the research team, which includes a post COVID-19 condition consumer (Appendix 1). The workbook headings are: Who I Really Am, Looking Beyond This Illness, My Best Day, Becoming the Person I Really Am, Physical Wellbeing, Communication, Emotional Wellbeing, Information Needs, Financial Issues, My Support Network, Thinking Issues, and Looking After My Own Health. Charge is grounded in well-established evidence, including self-determination theory, which proposes that intrinsic motivation composed of purpose, autonomy, mastery, and connectedness is necessary for successful outcomes [11,12,21]. By gently reflecting the participant’s own thoughts, Take Charge attempts to avoid shaping their goals, a process that can occur in traditional therapist-led goal setting [19].

Participants will receive their first Take Charge session within 2 weeks of randomization, followed by a second session 4 weeks later. Sessions will be delivered either in person or by using telehealth by an occupational therapist who is trained in the Take Charge approach and is not involved in delivering the control group. Sessions are designed to take up to 60 min but may finish earlier if the workbook is completed or if the participant wishes to stop. Sessions may also be extended to a maximum of 75 min, if deemed necessary by the therapist. If a participant reports symptoms such as chest pain, major depressive symptoms, or new neurological symptoms, they are directed to seek appropriate care. Due to the personal nature of the workbook, the participant will keep it as part of the intervention; it will not be collected or analyzed.

sec.title: Control: telephone follow up
sec.p: The control group will receive two telephone calls of up to 60 min each from an occupational therapist to match the time received by the Take Charge intervention group. This may also be extended up to a maximum of 75 min if deemed necessary by the therapist or shorter if the participant wishes. Participants will receive their first phone call within two weeks of randomization, followed by a second phone call four weeks later. During the telephone call the participant is given the opportunity to discuss post COVID-19 related symptoms. The content is standardised using a detailed script with clear written prompts and responses to common symptoms the patient may report. The advice provided is general in nature and in line with currently available COVID-19 guidelines. The call is delivered by an occupational therapist who has no prior training in the Take Charge approach and unlike the Take Charge approach, the patient will not be prompted to identify goals and strategies or be provided with a workbook thereby preventing any contamination. Additionally, if the participant wishes to discuss concerns outside the predefined scope of the control protocol they will be advised this is not possible during the session and directed to appropriate services if required. If concerning issues are identified during the phone call, appropriate actions will be taken; for example, directing the participant to see their general practitioner, attending the emergency department, or booking a follow-up PASC clinic appointment.

sec.title: Patient and public involvement
sec.p: The study invited a post COVID-19 condition consumer, identified through the PASC clinic, to join the research team as a co-investigator. As a co-investigator, the consumer has been involved from the initial grant application and beyond, providing valuable insight that has informed study design as well as the Take Charge workbook adaptation. They will continue to be involved in data analysis until the final publication.

sec.title: Primary study outcomes
sec.p: The two primary study outcomes are the Modified COVID-19 Yorkshire Rehabilitation Scale (17 items) [22] and the COVID-19 Core Outcome Measure for Recovery (1 item) [23]. The Modified COVID-19 Yorkshire Rehabilitation Scale was chosen because it is an updated condition-specific, reliable, validated, and responsive measure [24–27]. It captures domains of symptom severity, functional ability, overall health, employment, and the perspectives of family members or caregivers [22]. The COVID-19 Core Outcome Measure for Recovery was chosen because it is a non-burdensome single-item measure that provides an overall assessment of recovery that is relevant [23] and supported by a Delphi consensus [28].

sec.title: Secondary study outcomes
sec.p: The secondary physical and self-report study outcomes are outlined in Table 1, along with the corresponding schedule. Physical measures include the 6 min Walk Test [29], 1 min Sit to Stand test [30,31], and hand grip strength [31].

Self-report measures include the Medical Outcomes Study Short Form 36 (36 items), which was chosen as it is a well-established measure [32] encompassing eight subscales and has been used in previous post COVID-19 condition literature [33]. Dyspnea will be measured using the accepted Modified Medical Research Council Dyspnea Scale (1 item) [34], fatigue using the Fatigue Severity Scale (10 items) [35], and health-related self-efficacy using the General Self Efficacy Scale (10 items) [36,37]. Employment and economic loss will be measured using the Work Productivity and Activity Impairment Questionnaire (6 items) [38], as well as a survey of employment status and hours worked per week prior to COVID-19 at baseline, 3 months, and 6 months. The impact of post COVID-19 condition on mental health will be measured using the well-established Generalized Anxiety Disorder-7 (seven items) [39] and Patient Health Questionnaire-9 (nine items) [40]. These outcome measures are supported by international COVID-19 outcome consensus studies [5,23,28].

sec.title: Collection of outcome measures
sec.p: Objective physical outcome measures will be collected at baseline and at 3 months by a clinician blinded to the allocation. The primary study outcomes and other self-reported measures will be collected at baseline and at 3, 6, and 12 months. The 6-month data point will be considered the study endpoint, after which participants will have the option of receiving the alternate program, if desired. The study will endeavour to collect optional measures at 12 months, acknowledging that some respondents may have opted to receive alternate intervention in the interim, which will be accounted for in the analysis. The 6 month study endpoint was chosen as previous Take Charge literature [11] has demonstrated improvements as early as 6 months, this was determined to be a less burdensome and more realistic follow up period given the complex needs of this cohort and pilot design with lower likelihood of patients lost to follow up.

sec.title: Self-report measures
sec.p: Prior to the intake clinic, participants will be given the option of completing the self-report outcome measures online via the REDCap database or paper copy. The end self-report measures will be completed in the same format as the baseline to maintain consistency.

sec.title: Variations in collection timeframes
sec.p: If a participant is unable to attend an assessment or intervention session within a specified time period, there are predetermined acceptable protocol deviations, as outlined in Table 1. If a participant requires rescheduling outside the pre-accepted deviations, the research team will review the individual case, and if appropriate, will rebook the participant and submit a protocol deviation.

sec.title: Qualitative data collection
sec.p: The research team will purposively invite 10 participants from the Take Charge group and 5 from the control group to participate in a semi-structured qualitative interview after completion of their 3-month outcome measures to understand their experience of receiving the program and guide future studies. To capture a broad range of experiences and perspectives, the study will purposefully sample participants with varying levels of engagement and satisfaction with the program. Participants will be nominated by intervention and control clinicians who have an existing relationship with them. A sample size of 15 was selected based on feasibility – given the overall small anticipated recruitment and supported by previous literature indicating that small, purposeful samples with focused interviews are adequate to achieve data saturation [41–43]. Additional interviews will be conducted if data saturation is not achieved. The study will also interview therapists delivering the Take Charge and control group interventions. Interviews will be conducted in person or via the telephone. Data will be audio recorded, professionally transcribed, and coded using Braun & Clarke’s six step thematic analysis [44] to categorize and present key themes using an inductive approach. Two primary researchers will code all interviews separately, a third researcher will code a random 30% sample of the interviews for validity. All researchers will then meet and discuss their findings and come to a consensus of defining and naming common themes. Findings from the qualitative interviews will inform an explanatory mixed methods design, in which qualitative themes will be used to develop meta-inferences that help explain the quantitative outcomes and provide deeper insight into participants’ experiences with the Take Charge intervention – insights that are unlikely to be captured through standard outcome measures alone.

sec.title: Participant recruitment
sec.p: To estimate the recruitment achievable in a larger trial, the study will keep recruitment logs outlining the number of potential participants who are mailed a copy of the recruitment brochure and study details, the proportion that proceeds to consent, and those who decline with reasons for non-participation where possible. Recruitment logs will also identify the number of participants who meet the eligibility criteria and reasons for exclusion.

sec.title: Participant retention and outcome measures burden
sec.p: We will report the proportion of participants who completed the baseline and 3- and 6-month outcome measures in part or in full. The study will report on the proportion and reasons for attrition.

sec.title: Fidelity
sec.p: For each participant in the Take Charge group, the following will be measured when assessing fidelity [1]: receive at least one session of Take Charge by a trained occupational therapist [2], minimum session duration of 20 min [3], identified at least one item to work on, and [4] the therapist delivering Take Charge was not involved in delivering the control group. For each participant in the control group, the following will be measured when assessing fidelity [1]: receive at least one telephone follow-up [2], minimum phone call duration of 20 min [3], identified at least one symptom or concern to discuss, and [4] the therapist delivering the control group was not involved in delivering the Take Charge intervention.

sec.title: Intervention and control acceptability
sec.p: In addition to the qualitative interviews, the study will use Likert-type survey questions to assess the acceptability of the intervention and control. We will also assess participation, including mode, length of session, and participation in both sessions, as a marker of acceptability.

sec.title: Intervention efficacy
sec.p: Statistical analysis (intention-to-treat, multivariate mixed-effect linear regression model) will be conducted for each outcome to evaluate significant and clinically meaningful changes in symptoms, physical measures, and quality of life.

sec.title: Health economics
sec.p: The study will calculate the cost of providing the Take Charge intervention and use Medical Outcomes Short Form Health Survey 36 responses to estimate utility weights to inform intervention effects on quality-adjusted life years. Utilizing data from the Work Productivity and Activity Impairment questionnaire and survey questions regarding work status and hours worked per week, this study will report the impact of the intervention on productivity.

sec.title: Progression criteria
sec.p: The criteria deemed to be supportive of a subsequent larger, multicentre trial are outlined in Table 2. This approach has been adapted from a previous study [45] and cut offs listed are in line with previous expert panel recommendations [46]. These criteria will be considered in conjunction with qualitative interview findings.

caption.p: Progression criteria for a larger multicentre trial.

tr.td: ≥ 70% of participants complete primary outcome measures at 6-month endpoint
40–69% complete primary outcome measures through at 6-month endpoint
<40% complete primary outcome measures through to the 6-month endpoint
≥ 80% of participants report the intervention was acceptable
The intervention showed evidence of effect in 2 or more outcome measures
The intervention showed evidence of effect in 1 or more outcome measures
The intervention showed no evidence of effect

sec.title: Adverse events
sec.p: Harms and unintended effects will be monitored through patient self-report and outcome measures. These will be recorded and evaluated with the trial Adverse Events Committee and escalated to the overseeing Southern Adelaide Clinical Human Research Ethics Committee as appropriate in line with their guidelines.

sec.title: Non-participation or withdrawal
sec.p: Using an intention-to-treat approach, researchers will attempt to obtain outcome measures at 3, 6, and 12 (optional) months from all randomized participants, regardless of whether they participated or deviated from the intervention or control sessions. If this is not possible, a record will be made that the participant was lost to follow-up for a reason if identified.

Participants will be able to fully withdraw at any time. The research team may also discontinue participants if this is deemed necessary. Participants who withdraw will have data collected until the point of withdrawal unless they request their information to be removed.

sec.title: Sample size calculation
sec.p: At the time of initial study design, the primary outcome measure – the Modified COVID-19 Yorkshire Rehabilitation Scale was only very recently released and hence the authors were unable to find published mean and standard deviations for a similar population to inform this study’s sample size calculation. The authors will perform a sample size calculation once translatable published data is available for the Modified COVID-19 Yorkshire Rehabilitation Scale including mean, standard deviation and minimal important difference. If this information is not available the study will consider performing a pre-planned blinded sample size calculation using the baseline Modified COVID-19 Yorkshire Rehabilitation Scale scores of the first 40 recruited participants to calculate the mean and standard deviation in order to determine the final recruitment target. This method has been established previously [47].

sec.title: Statistical analyses
sec.p: All statistical analyses will be conducted using R version 4.3.2 [48]. Participant characteristics will be expressed as median and interquartile range (IQR) for skewed data. The Mann-Whitney U test will be used to explore the significance of differences in participant characteristics between the control and intervention groups. Proportions will be presented as percentages of the respective denominator and will be compared between groups using a standard chi-square test for association with continuity correction, where appropriate. A multivariable mixed-effect linear regression model (two-level random slope model) will be used for each outcome due to the hierarchical structure of the data (outcome occurs for each participant with repeated time points). This approach allows for the inclusion of both fixed and random effects and accounts for within-subject correlation by including a random intercept for each subject. Time will be treated as a fixed effect (baseline, 3 and 6 months) to accommodate for potential non-linear trends over time. Random slopes for time will be included at the participant level to account for individual variability in outcome trajectories. Fixed effects will capture the group effect (control or intervention), time effect (modelled as a categorical variable), and group-by-time interaction, while participant-level random effects will account for within-subject variability. Initial analyses involved univariable models, followed by multivariable models by adding variables considered clinically important or statistically significant from the univariate model to adjust for confounding effects between variables. Model parameters will be estimated using the maximum likelihood estimation procedure, and model diagnostics and goodness of fit will be evaluated using the likelihood ratio test. Two-sided tests will be performed and the level of significance will be set at α = 0.05. Multiple imputation analysis will also be performed to account for missing data, specifically for outcomes at the later time point of the study.

sec.title: Ethics
sec.p: This study adheres to the Declaration of Helsinki. This study was approved by the Southern Adelaide Clinical Human Research Ethics Committee (approval number: 2022/SSA00695/OFR: 219.22, protocol version 3.3 19 February 2024).

sec.title: Data monitoring
sec.p: A strict process of quality control and secure record-keeping will be implemented in accordance with ethical approval. All study participants will be assigned a unique, de-identified number. Participant information from the intake and 3-month face-to-face appointments will be stored in their individual hospital electronic medical records system. In addition, all participant information will be securely stored in a password-protected electronic information management database (REDCap) and in secure documents and folders on the local health network computer drives. Files containing identifying information will be password-protected, and only the researchers involved in the trial will have access to the database. Two-factor authentication will be required to access the REDCap database, with relevant restrictions applied to approved users to ensure that participant privacy is maintained. Paper files will be stored in a locked cabinet in the research offices located at the Flinders Medical Centre, Rehabilitation Aged, and Palliative Division.

A Standard Operating Procedure has been developed for daily use. Day-to-day management of the trial is the responsibility of the Chief Investigator (SL) and will be overseen by the trial management group. The trial management group will meet regularly to assess progress, discuss any issues, and be kept up to date by email.

sec.title: Dissemination
sec.p: The results (including amendments to the study protocol) will be disseminated in peer-reviewed journals and conference presentations. A plain language summary will be posted to trial participants interested in receiving the study results. We also present the findings in the media (social and mainstream).

sec.title: Trial status
sec.p: The first participant was randomized to the trial on the 22nd of December 2023. Recruitment is ongoing.

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: This pragmatic randomized trial will adapt and evaluate the efficacy and acceptability of the Take Charge intervention in people with post COVID-19 condition. Given the widespread and profoundly debilitating nature of post COVID-19 condition with no curative treatment, this study will provide knowledge in this area of evidence and hopes to inform future practice and policy.

Functional, psychological, economic, and quality of life outcomes will provide information on the impact of interventions on individuals and the health system. This study has several key strengths, including the intervention being low risk, low cost, and practical implementation with the potential for scale-up across health services. The trial has very few exclusion criteria, representing a real-world cohort with generalizable results, and the mixed-method evaluation will provide useful feasibility and acceptability data. Randomization will be centralized with blinded physical outcome measures and stringent data collection and storage processes using an electronic database. The study will measure a range of outcomes, post COVID-19 condition specific, broader objective, and self-report measures, as well as feasibility and acceptability outcomes with a qualitative study embedded to provide robust information and guide a larger trial if required. While the single centre recruitment limits generalisability of results can still inform practice and a larger follow up study. The inability to blind participants to their group allocation and the short 6 month follow up period which may not capture longer term quality of life improvements are noted limitations. While recruitment from a hospital clinic with broad eligibility criteria will demonstrate whether this program could be integrated into routine care, a single-centre recruitment approach reduces external validity.

ack.title: Acknowledgements
ack.p: The study would like to acknowledge the participants involved. The study would also like to acknowledge clinicians including Sarah Clapp, Matt Lo Basso, Jack Davison and Rachel Dew, as well as support from Ruby Chan and Emma Starfield.

sec.title: Disclosure statement
sec.p: Author H.M. was in the research team for the Take Charge after Stroke randomized trials. He has no commercial interest in Take Charge materials (they are free) or training (also free).

sec.title: Ethical approval
sec.p: This study adheres to the Declaration of Helsinki. Southern Adelaide Clinical Human Research Ethics Committee approval number: 2022/SSA00695/OFR: 219.22. Contact details: Telephone: +61 08 8204 6453/www.sahealth.sa.gov.au/SALHNresearch

sec.title: Data availability statement
sec.p: The data that support the findings of this study are available upon request from the corresponding author [SL]. The data are not publicly available due to restrictions, for example, containing information that could compromise the privacy of research participants.

ref.mixed-citation: data.who.int WHO. WHO coronavirus (COVID-19) dashboard > About [dashboard]; 2023.
Flatby AV, Himmels JPW, Brurberg KG, et al. COVID-19: post COVID-19 condition-a rapid review; 2022.
Davis HE, McCorkell L, Vogel JM, et al. Long COVID: major findings, mechanisms, and recommendations. Nat Rev Microbiol. 2023;21(3):133–146. doi: 10.1038/s41579-022-00846-2.36639608 PMC9839201
